    Home › Aircraft › Boeing 727

    Boeing 727

    Boeing · Trijet · Retired from airline service

    The jet that opened the small airports.

    Length46.69 m
    Wingspan32.92 m
    Range4,020 km
    MTOW95 t
    Typical seats155
    Built1,832
    Our numbers

    Derived metrics

    Computed by aircraft.fyi from the core specification — see how we calculate these.

    Seats per metre#24 of 30
    3.32 seats/m typical seats ÷ fuselage length
    Range per tonne#22 of 40
    42.3 km/t maximum range ÷ MTOW in tonnes
    Span-to-length ratio#39 of 43
    0.71 wingspan ÷ fuselage length
    Weight per metre#38 of 43
    2.04 t/m MTOW ÷ fuselage length
    Range in hours#36 of 40
    4.2 h maximum range ÷ cruise speed
    Weight per seat#21 of 30
    613 kg MTOW ÷ typical seats
    Sense of scale

    How big is it, really?

    The Boeing 727's wingspan, against things you already know the size of.

    Boeing 72732.92 m
    Football pitch (width)68 m
    Blue whale25 m
    New Routemaster bus11.2 m
    You (probably)1.75 m
    #35 of 43 by length → #37 by weight → #9 by speed →
    Specification

    The numbers

    Length (727-200)46.69 m
    Wingspan32.92 m
    Height10.36 m
    MTOW (-200 Adv)95,028 kg
    Range≈4,020 km
    Typical seats (-200)155 (up to 189)
    Max speed953 km/h (Mach 0.90)
    Engines3 × P&W JT8D, rear-mounted
    First flight9 February 1963
    Built1,832

    Compare it

    Put the Boeing 727 next to something

    vs Lockheed L-1011 →vs DC-10 →
    vs Boeing 737 vs DC-10 Compare with anything →
    ← PreviousATR 72 Next →Boeing 737

    Last verified: 2026-07-14 · Spot an error? business@luck.fyi

    The story

    What makes it different

    The 727 was designed to bring jets to airports that could not handle them: short runways, no ground equipment, thin markets. Boeing gave it a remarkable high-lift wing with triple-slotted flaps, three engines clustered at the tail so the wing stayed clean, and airstairs built into the rear so it could turn around anywhere.

    It worked so well that it became the first jetliner to pass 1,000 sales, and 1,832 were built — a record that stood until the 737 passed it. For two decades it was the sound of American aviation.

    Noise rules and fuel prices retired it from the airlines, and the famous rear airstair was locked shut after D.B. Cooper parachuted out of one in 1971. A handful still fly as freighters and government aircraft.

    Variants

    The Boeing 727 line

    727-100
    The 40.6 m original.
    727-200 / -200 Adv
    The 46.69 m stretch — most of the fleet. Spec shown above.
    727F / Super 27
    Freighter and re-engined conversions — the last flyers.
    Operators

    Who flies the Boeing 727

    No current operators — this aircraft's flying days are over. The operators of record are below.

    USCargo and government operatorsA handful of freighters, VIP transports and special-mission aircraft remain airworthy worldwide.≈10

    About the data. Every specification is compiled from manufacturer documents and cited per row. Operator fleet counts are approximate snapshots, marked ≈ and dated; individually verified figures carry a ✓. All silhouettes are original works of aircraft.fyi, drawn to true scale. Corrections and enquiries: business@luck.fyi — we fix errors visibly, not silently.
